title: Model-benchmark runs write reports without the reducer ledger row
detail: The non-reviewer model-benchmark command invokes loop-host without --state-log while using an outputs dir outside any improvement/ ancestor. run-benchmark.cjs only appends benchmark_run when args['state-log'] is provided or inferStateLogPath(outputsDir) finds an ancestor named improvement, so normal runs can produce report.json but leave agent-improvement-state.jsonl without the row reduce-state consumes.
claim: Normal non-reviewer /deep:model-benchmark runs can silently omit the benchmark_run ledger record, causing reduce-state to undercount benchmark runs and stop-status inputs.
counterevidenceSought: I checked the loop-host option forwarding and the model-benchmark YAML commands. loop-host forwards state-log only if it receives one, and the non-reviewer YAML branches do not pass it; only the reviewer-scorer branch includes an explicit --state-log.
alternativeExplanation: A human verification action could catch the missing row, but the configured command itself still omits the path and auto mode has no executable fail-closed guard in this snippet.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the command renderer injects --state-log into the loop-host invocation outside the YAML text shown here, or if reduce-state no longer consumes agent-improvement-state.jsonl for benchmark accounting.
suggestedFix: Pass --state-log {spec_folder}/improvement/agent-improvement-state.jsonl through both model-benchmark loop-host commands, or make loop-host derive and require the ledger path for model-benchmark mode.

title: Workflow promotion still defaults to immediate canonical mutation
detail: promote-candidate.cjs implements accept and ship phases, but normalizePhase defaults a missing --phase to promote and the legacy promote branch still copies candidate over target directly. The normal promotion commands in agent-improvement confirm and model-benchmark auto/confirm omit --phase, so selecting the accepted-candidate option does not create an acceptance file or run the ship precondition path.
claim: The accepted-vs-shipped safety split is bypassed in the normal workflow commands because they invoke the promoter without --phase.
counterevidenceSought: I checked the promotion command strings in the workflow YAML and the promoter's phase dispatch. None of the normal command strings pass --phase=accept or --phase=ship.
alternativeExplanation: The default promote phase may be kept for backward compatibility, but the current workflow text says accepted candidate/variant and does not opt into the new accept/ship contract.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if these YAML commands are not used by the shipped workflows, or if an outer orchestration layer rewrites them into explicit accept and ship invocations before execution.
suggestedFix: Update workflows to create an acceptance file with --phase=accept and ship with --phase=ship --acceptance-file=..., or make --phase mandatory so accidental legacy mutation fails closed.

title: Pre-mutation mirror sync blocks legitimate agent-definition promotion
detail: For agent-definition targets, promote-candidate.cjs reads the candidate content and runs verifyMirrorSync before the phase-specific copy. verifyMirrorSync compares that candidate body against the current .opencode/.claude/.codex agent mirrors, including the target path itself. In a normal accept/ship or legacy promote where the candidate differs from the current target, the mirror gate rejects the change before the accepted snapshot is copied.
claim: Agent-definition promotion can fail before mutation even when the candidate is valid, because the all-mirrors-in-sync check is performed against the future candidate before any mirror has been updated.
counterevidenceSought: I checked the test coverage for accept/ship; it uses temporary non-agent target files, so it does not exercise isAgentDefinitionTarget or the mirror-sync branch.
alternativeExplanation: If the intended workflow requires all mirrors to be pre-updated before promotion, then this helper should not present itself as copying a candidate into a canonical target; as written, the copy happens after the gate that requires the copy's result.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if deep-improvement never promotes .opencode/.claude/.codex agent definitions, or if candidates are always pre-landed into every runtime mirror before promote-candidate.cjs runs.
suggestedFix: Split mirror handling into preflight and post-copy checks: verify current mirrors before mutation if needed, copy/stage the accepted candidate into the target or all mirrors, then run the all-in-sync gate on the resulting files.

title: Rollback ignores the stored accepted-state hash before restoring backup
detail: The acceptance state records status and preAcceptTargetHash, but rollback-candidate.cjs does not check either before copying acceptedState.preAcceptBackupPath over the canonical target. A stale or modified backup file under the archive path will be restored as if it were the verified pre-acceptance target.
claim: Rollback can silently corrupt the canonical target if the accepted-state backup file is modified, because the stored hash is available but unused.
counterevidenceSought: I checked rollback-candidate.cjs for any sha256 verification or acceptedState.status guard and found only path existence plus manifest/config target checks.
alternativeExplanation: The archive directory may be treated as trusted, but this is a recovery path and the acceptance file already records the exact hash needed to verify the backup before mutation.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if archive backups are immutable by construction in the runtime environment, or if rollback is never invoked from acceptance files.
suggestedFix: When --acceptance-file is used, require acceptedState.status === 'accepted' and verify sha256(backup) equals acceptedState.preAcceptTargetHash before copying; fail closed on mismatch.

title: Hub registry packet-name check reads the wrong schema location
detail: scanHubRegistry checks mode.packetSkillName, but the live mode-registry schema stores packetSkillName under advisorRouting. The synthetic test fixture uses the top-level shape, so it does not catch the shipped-schema mismatch. A live packet-name drift in advisorRouting.packetSkillName would not be reported by this guard.
claim: The D5 hub registry packet-name mismatch guard is ineffective for the live registry schema.
counterevidenceSought: I checked the live registry entries and the test helper shape. The live entries nest packetSkillName in advisorRouting, while the synthetic registry places it at top level.
alternativeExplanation: Some other hub registry may still use the top-level field, but the shipped deep-loop registry shape shown here is nested and would bypass this branch.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the intended D5 contract only supports older top-level packetSkillName registries and does not claim coverage for advisorRouting.packetSkillName.
suggestedFix: Resolve expected packet name from mode.packetSkillName ?? mode.advisorRouting?.packetSkillName and add a test fixture using the nested live registry shape.
